subject: ap-government
title: Quantitative Analysis — Popular Vote Share in Presidential Elections by Party
stimulus: Source: Dave Leip's Atlas of U.S. Presidential Elections, 2016. Bar graph showing Percent of Popular Vote Share in Presidential Elections by Party for Democratic, Republican, and Third-Party/Independent candidates across presidential elections.

parts.prompt: Identify the year in which a presidential candidate had the greatest percent of the popular vote in a presidential election.

scoring_points.description: Identify the year in which a presidential candidate had the greatest percent of the popular vote in a presidential election.

required_elements: Identifies 1972 as the year with the greatest popular vote percentage for any candidate
alternatives.correct_example: 1972 was the year in which a presidential candidate had the greatest percent of the popular vote.

wrong_examples: 1984 — Ronald Reagan won by a large margin in 1984 but not as large as Nixon's 1972 landslide.
1964 — Lyndon Johnson won by a large margin but less than Nixon's 1972 share.
common_traps: The answer must be based on the data in the specific bar graph provided — only 1972 earns the point
Accept '1972' or 'Nixon in 1972' — either phrasing is acceptable
scoring_points.official_rubric: One point is earned for identifying the year in which a presidential candidate had the greatest percent of the popular vote. Acceptable response: The year in which a presidential candidate had the greatest percent was 1972.

sample_responses.response: 1972 was the year in which a presidential candidate had the greatest percentage of the popular vote.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly identifies 1972 (Nixon's landslide win) as the year with the greatest popular vote share per the graph. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: 1984, when Reagan won by a very large margin.
sample_responses.commentary: Reagan's 1984 margin was large, but the data in the graph shows 1972 had the greatest percentage. Answer must be based on the provided graph data. 0 pts.

parts.prompt: Describe the overall trend in the percent of the popular vote of third-party/independent candidates.
parts.scoring_notes: Accept any accurate description of the overall pattern for third-party candidates across the elections shown. Must describe a trend or pattern, not just a single data point.

scoring_points.description: Describe the overall trend in the percent of the popular vote of third-party/independent candidates.

required_elements: Accurately describes the overall pattern for third-party/independent candidates across the elections shown
alternatives.correct_example: Third-party and independent candidates consistently receive a much smaller share of the popular vote than Democratic or Republican candidates — generally under 10% — with occasional spikes such as Ross Perot's performance in 1992.
The overall trend shows that third-party candidates rarely receive more than 20% of the popular vote and in most elections receive very little support compared to the two major parties.

wrong_examples: Third-party candidates are growing in popularity — this overstates what the data shows; the overall pattern is one of limited support with occasional spikes.
Describing only the 1992 election without characterizing the overall pattern.
common_traps: Must describe a trend across multiple elections — not just describe one data point
Accept any accurate characterization of the low and limited third-party vote share as a pattern
scoring_points.official_rubric: One point is earned for describing the overall trend in the percent of the popular vote of third-party/independent candidates. Acceptable descriptions include: Third-party candidates do not get as high a percentage of the popular vote as major party candidates. Third-party candidates never win 20% of the popular vote. Third-party candidates never win a majority of the popular vote.

sample_responses.response: Third-party and independent candidates consistently receive a much smaller share of the popular vote than Democratic or Republican candidates — generally well below 20% — with only occasional spikes such as in 1992.
sample_responses.commentary: Accurately describes the overall pattern across elections (consistently low third-party vote share) rather than just one data point. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: In 1992, Ross Perot received about 19% of the popular vote.
sample_responses.commentary: Describes a single data point rather than an overall trend across the elections shown in the graph. 0 pts.

parts.prompt: Using the data, draw a conclusion about how the third-party/independent candidate may have affected the outcome of the 1992 presidential election.
parts.scoring_notes: The response must draw a conclusion about impact — not just note that Perot received votes. Accept any plausible inference about how the third-party vote could have affected the electoral outcome.

scoring_points.description: Using the data, draw a conclusion about how the third-party/independent candidate may have affected the outcome of the 1992 presidential election.

required_elements: Draws a specific conclusion about how the 1992 third-party candidate's vote share may have affected the election outcome
alternatives.correct_example: In 1992, Ross Perot received approximately 19% of the popular vote — an unusually high share for a third-party candidate. This likely drew votes away from one or both major party candidates, potentially changing the outcome in closely contested states and influencing the final electoral college result.

required_elements: Draws a conclusion about the impact of the 1992 third-party vote
alternatives.correct_example: The large third-party vote share in 1992 suggests Perot may have acted as a 'spoiler,' drawing votes that would otherwise have gone to one major party candidate, potentially costing that candidate the election. The winner in 1992 may have won with a smaller share of the popular vote than usual due to the divided vote.

wrong_examples: Ross Perot received many votes — this describes the data but does not draw a conclusion about the election outcome.
Perot won the election — Perot did not win; the conclusion must be about how his vote share affected the major party outcome.
common_traps: The response must go beyond describing the data to inferring an effect on the outcome
Accept conclusions from either direction — that Perot helped Clinton win or helped other interpretations — as long as the reasoning connects to the data
scoring_points.official_rubric: One point is earned for drawing a conclusion about how the third-party/independent candidate may have affected the outcome of the 1992 presidential election. Acceptable conclusions include: In 1992, the third-party candidate received a significant number of votes, and if those votes were allocated differently, they could have affected the outcome of the election by changing the number of electors won in the electoral college.

sample_responses.response: In 1992, the third-party candidate received a significant share of the popular vote. If those votes had been cast for one of the major party candidates instead, they could have changed the outcome of the election by shifting the electoral college results in several closely contested states.
sample_responses.commentary: Goes beyond describing the data to draw a conclusion about how the third-party vote share affected the electoral outcome. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: Ross Perot received about 19% of the popular vote in 1992.
sample_responses.commentary: Describes the data without drawing a conclusion about its effect on the election outcome. 0 pts.

parts.prompt: Explain why the data in the bar graph does not necessarily relate to the outcome of presidential elections.
parts.scoring_notes: The response must explain the electoral college as the mechanism that separates popular vote share from election outcomes.

scoring_points.description: Explain why the data in the bar graph does not necessarily relate to the outcome of presidential elections.

required_elements: Explains that the Electoral College, not the popular vote, determines the winner of presidential elections
alternatives.correct_example: The bar graph shows popular vote percentages, but presidential elections are decided by the Electoral College. A candidate can win the popular vote but lose the election if they do not win enough states to secure 270 electoral votes — as happened in 2000 and 2016.

required_elements: Explains how the winner-take-all Electoral College system disconnects popular vote from electoral outcomes
alternatives.correct_example: Because most states use a winner-take-all system for allocating electoral votes, the candidate who wins the most votes nationwide does not necessarily win the election. A candidate who wins many large states by narrow margins can accumulate more electoral votes than a candidate who wins the popular vote by running up large margins in fewer states.

wrong_examples: Not everyone votes — voter turnout is not the reason popular vote doesn't determine the winner; the Electoral College is.
Polls are inaccurate — accuracy of polls is irrelevant to the question of why popular vote share doesn't determine presidential election outcomes.
common_traps: The key explanation must be about the Electoral College — not about polling, turnout, or other factors
Accept references to the winner-take-all system or the possibility of winning the Electoral College without the popular vote
scoring_points.official_rubric: One point is earned for explaining why the data in the bar graph does not necessarily relate to the outcome of presidential elections. Acceptable explanations include: The data in the graph do not relate to the outcome because the electoral college, not the popular vote, determines the winner. The electoral college allows states to choose how they allocate their votes for determining state electors. Most states use a winner-take-all system which sometimes leads to the candidate with the most popular votes not winning the election.

sample_responses.response: The bar graph shows popular vote percentages, but presidential elections are determined by the Electoral College, not the popular vote. A candidate can win the popular vote nationwide but lose the election if they fail to secure enough electoral votes — as occurred in 2000 and 2016.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly identifies the Electoral College as the mechanism that disconnects popular vote share from election outcomes. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: Not everyone votes, so popular vote data is not perfectly accurate.
sample_responses.commentary: Voter turnout is not the reason popular vote doesn't determine the winner — the Electoral College is. This misidentifies the mechanism. 0 pts.
